concepts.lesson: A triangle is a simple closed geometric figure formed by joining three non-collinear points using three line segments. These segments form three sides and enclose a region with three angles and three vertices. Triangles are the simplest polygons and serve as the basic building blocks for many geometric shapes. They are rigid structures, which is why triangles are widely used in construction and engineering designs.
concepts.real_example: A triangular road sign used to indicate warnings.

concepts.concept_name: Types of Triangles Based on Sides
concepts.lesson: Based on the lengths of their sides, triangles are classified as scalene, isosceles, or equilateral. A scalene triangle has all sides of different lengths, an isosceles triangle has two sides equal, and an equilateral triangle has all three sides equal. This classification helps in identifying symmetry and understanding properties such as equality of angles and sides in triangles.
concepts.real_example: An equilateral triangle used in decorative tile patterns.

concepts.concept_name: Types of Triangles Based on Angles
concepts.lesson: Triangles can also be classified based on the measures of their interior angles. An acute triangle has all angles less than 90°, a right-angled triangle has exactly one angle equal to 90°, and an obtuse triangle has one angle greater than 90°. This classification is important for understanding properties related to trigonometry and for selecting appropriate formulas in problem-solving.
concepts.real_example: A ladder leaning against a wall forming a right-angled triangle.

concepts.concept_name: Angle Sum Property of a Triangle
concepts.lesson: The angle sum property of a triangle states that the sum of the measures of the three interior angles of a triangle is always equal to 180 degrees. This property holds true for all types of triangles, regardless of their shape or size. It is widely used to find unknown angles in triangles and serves as a foundational result for proving other geometric properties.
concepts.real_example: Finding an unknown angle in a triangular frame.

concepts.lesson: The triangle inequality property states that the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the length of the third side. This condition is necessary and sufficient for three line segments to form a triangle. It is used to verify whether a given set of side lengths can form a triangle and plays an important role in solving geometry and construction-based problems.
concepts.real_example: Checking whether three sticks can form a triangular tent frame.
key_points: Sum of any two sides > third side
Invalid side sets cannot form triangles
